---
title: "UK Housing Affordability Monitor"
---

```{python}
import json
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

metadata = json.loads(Path("data/metadata.json").read_text())
trend = pd.read_csv("data/affordability_trend.csv")
latest = pd.read_csv("data/affordability_by_area.csv")
latest = latest[(latest["year"] == metadata["latest_year"]) & (latest["area_type"] == "local_authority")]
least = pd.read_csv("data/least_affordable_latest.csv")
most = pd.read_csv("data/most_affordable_latest.csv")
```

::: {.hero}
# Housing affordability, automated from official statistics

This monitor combines ONS house price statistics and ASHE earnings data to track how many years of median gross annual pay are implied by the median local house price in England and Wales.
:::

::: {.stat-grid}
```{python}
#| output: asis
print(f"""
<div class="stat-card">
  <div class="label">Latest year</div>
  <div class="value">{metadata['latest_year']}</div>
  <div class="small">September year-ending house price period aligned to annual earnings.</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
  <div class="label">Median ratio</div>
  <div class="value">{metadata['median_ratio_latest']}x</div>
  <div class="small">Median local authority house-price-to-earnings ratio.</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
  <div class="label">Least affordable</div>
  <div class="value">{metadata['least_affordable_area']['affordability_ratio']}x</div>
  <div class="small">{metadata['least_affordable_area']['area_name']}</div>
</div>
<div class="stat-card">
  <div class="label">Areas monitored</div>
  <div class="value">{metadata['area_count_latest']}</div>
  <div class="small">Local authorities with matched ONS house price and earnings data.</div>
</div>
""")
```
:::

```{python}
fig = px.line(
    trend,
    x="year",
    y="affordability_ratio",
    markers=True,
    title="Median local affordability ratio over time",
    labels={"year": "Year", "affordability_ratio": "Median house price / median annual pay"},
)
fig.update_traces(line_color="#123c69", marker_color="#f3b700", line_width=3)
fig.update_layout(height=430, margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=60, b=20))
fig.show()
```

```{python}
plot_latest = latest.sort_values("affordability_ratio", ascending=False).head(25)
fig = px.bar(
    plot_latest.sort_values("affordability_ratio"),
    x="affordability_ratio",
    y="area_name",
    orientation="h",
    title=f"Highest affordability ratios, {metadata['latest_year']}",
    labels={"affordability_ratio": "Ratio", "area_name": "Local authority"},
    color="affordability_ratio",
    color_continuous_scale="Blues",
)
fig.update_layout(height=720, margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=60, b=20), coloraxis_showscale=False)
fig.show()
```

## Interpretation

The indicator is deliberately simple: a value of `8.0` means the median house price is eight times median gross annual resident earnings in that local authority. This makes local differences easy to explain while keeping the data pipeline reproducible and auditable.

::: {.note}
This is not a full mortgage affordability model. It does not include deposits, interest rates, household composition, wealth, taxes, benefits, or housing quality. It is a clear headline indicator for monitoring spatial and temporal affordability pressure.
:::

## Most affordable areas

```{python}
most[["area_name", "median_house_price", "median_annual_pay", "affordability_ratio"]].rename(
    columns={
        "area_name": "Area",
        "median_house_price": "Median house price",
        "median_annual_pay": "Median annual pay",
        "affordability_ratio": "Ratio",
    }
).head(10)
```

## Least affordable areas

```{python}
least[["area_name", "median_house_price", "median_annual_pay", "affordability_ratio"]].rename(
    columns={
        "area_name": "Area",
        "median_house_price": "Median house price",
        "median_annual_pay": "Median annual pay",
        "affordability_ratio": "Ratio",
    }
).head(10)
```

```{python}
#| output: asis
print(f"<p class='small'>Last data refresh: {metadata['generated_at']}</p>")
```
